summary: Gruber provides a three-week update on his decision to go full-time with Daring Fireball, sharing a membership revenue graph and explaining his financial goal structure. He plans to fund the site through roughly equal thirds: memberships/t-shirts, advertising, and other initiatives. Revenue has reached about 80 percent of his initial membership drive goal. He then pivots to a broader observation about online monetization: people are far more willing to pay for physical goods (atoms) than digital content (bits), which explains why t-shirt sales dominate his revenue. He contrasts his freely-available content model with the New York Times's TimesSelect paywall, noting that even he was reluctant to pay $50/year for content he read daily.
key_points: Gruber's financial plan divides Daring Fireball revenue into roughly equal thirds: memberships/shirts, advertising, and other initiatives
T-shirt sales consistently outperform pure membership/donation revenue, illustrating the 'atoms vs. bits' principle
People have a deep psychological reluctance to pay for digital content even when the per-use cost is trivial
Gruber's model keeps all content free, making memberships voluntary support rather than gated access — a key distinction from paywalls
Even at 80 percent of his goal, Gruber is transparent about needing continued support, countering assumptions that he's already set
money_quotes: People are more willing to spend money for atoms than for bits.
You can't touch ones and zeroes.

Despite the fact that the reason you fine readers are buying these shirts is that you want to support my work, I don't think I'd be nearly as successful if I were only asking for donations or selling memberships.
This isn't criticism; it's just an observation about human nature.

$50 a year isn't a lot of money for something I read at least 300 times per year — it works out to like 15 cents a day — but yet I was reluctant to sign-up because it just didn't seem right to have to pay for something to read on the web.
That's what's so gratifying about the membership system here — readers aren't signing up because they have to. They're signing up because they want to.
key_insight: People will readily pay for physical goods to support digital work they value, but resist paying for digital content directly — a fundamental tension in online monetization that favors hybrid models over pure paywalls.
